## Học hỏi từ slopus/happy và happier-dev/happier: cách hook vào Claude Code, và bài học cho PTY-over-NetBird + libghostty

> Lưu ý trung thực về độ tin cậy: phần lớn nội dung dưới đây đã được verify bằng cách đọc source thật. Tôi đã clone lại `slopus/happy` để giải quyết một mâu thuẫn trong corpus và phát hiện **một claim trong corpus đã lỗi thời** — sẽ flag rõ ở mục 1. Các điểm còn `uncertain`/`refuted` từ verdict cũng được phản ánh honest trong từng phần.

---

### 1. slopus/happy móc vào Claude Code KIỂU GÌ

**Kiến trúc 3 tầng** (monorepo pnpm, `packages/`): `happy-cli` (host daemon) ↔ `happy-server` (relay Fastify + Socket.IO + Postgres/Redis/S3) ↔ `happy-app` (client Expo/RN + Tauri). Ngoài ra có `codium` (Electron app riêng) và `happy-wire` (protocol types). Relay là **bắt buộc**, KHÔNG có P2P.

**Hook mechanism — hai chế độ:**

- **LOCAL / interactive mode** (`happy-cli/src/claude/claudeLocal.ts:316`): spawn binary `claude` với `stdio: ['inherit','inherit','inherit','pipe']`. stdin/stdout/stderr **inherit thẳng** → full TUI hiện trong terminal của user, KHÔNG bị intercept byte. fd 3 (pipe thứ 4) là side-channel để bắt `fetch-start`/`fetch-end` (trạng thái "thinking"). Mobile KHÔNG nhận byte stream TUI — chỉ nhận structured events từ `sessionScanner` đọc file JSONL transcript Claude ghi ra đĩa. Đây là approach (a) + (c).

- **REMOTE / headless mode** (`happy-cli/src/claude/sdk/query.ts`): **ĐÍNH CHÍNH QUAN TRỌNG so với corpus.** Corpus point #1 khẳng định "spawns the claude binary — not the Anthropic Agent SDK library directly". Đọc source HIỆN TẠI cho thấy điều ngược lại: `query.ts:6` `import { query as sdkQuery } from '@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk'` và `query.ts:80` gọi thẳng `sdkQuery(...)`; `package.json` khai báo dep `"@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk": "^0.2.96"`. Tức happy hiện **dùng official Claude Agent SDK** (SDK này bên trong tự spawn `claude --output-format stream-json`), không còn tự dựng spawn thủ công. Claim cũ trong corpus là **lỗi thời** — đây chính là điểm verdict của happier (`@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` là public package, confirmed npm 201 versions) phản ánh đúng hơn. Đây là approach (b).

**Session ID tracking qua native hook:** `generateHookSettings.ts:34` ghi file tạm `~/.happy/tmp/hooks/session-hook-<pid>.json` chứa `hooks.SessionStart` trỏ tới `session_hook_forwarder.cjs <port>`, spawn `claude --settings <file>`. Khi Claude fire SessionStart, forwarder POST `{session_id, transcript_path, cwd}` về `127.0.0.1:<port>/hook/session-start` (`startHookServer.ts`). **Lưu ý:** happy hiện vẫn dùng `--settings` cho hook (xác nhận trong source) — chưa migrate sang `--plugin-dir`, đây là điểm happier khác happy (xem mục 2). Ngoài ra `startHappyServer()` inject một MCP HTTP server (`mcp__happy__*` qua `--mcp-config`/`--allowedTools`) để Claude gọi tool tương tác với happy layer.

**Transport / auth / encryption:**
- Transport: Socket.IO/WSS tới `wss://api.cluster-fluster.com/v1/updates` (production endpoint **confirmed** trong `configuration.ts:59` và `pushNotifications.ts`, không phải dev leftover). Webapp tại `app.happy.engineering`. Không P2P.
- Encryption: **E2E thật** — server chỉ thấy base64 blob opaque. Hai variant (`docs/encryption.md`): legacy NaCl `secretbox` (XSalsa20-Poly1305) và `dataKey` AES-256-GCM với per-session key wrap qua `tweetnacl.box`. **ĐÍNH CHÍNH (verdict: refuted):** việc chọn variant KHÔNG phải server push per-session; nó được quyết định **một lần lúc auth** dựa trên credential trong `~/.happy/access.key` (client gửi `supportsV2:true`, server trả 32-byte=legacy hoặc bundle prefix-0=dataKey). dataKey là de-facto default cho account mới trên server V2, nhưng không có cơ chế "server cấp dataKey mỗi session".
- Auth relay: keypair NaCl local → ký challenge → `POST /v1/auth {publicKey, challenge, signature}` → JWT Bearer. Không password/email.
- Auth Claude: `authenticateClaude.ts` chạy PKCE OAuth (`client_id=9d1c250a-e61b-44d9-88ed-5944d1962f5e`, `claude.ai/oauth/authorize`, scope `user:inference`); token reuse subscription Claude, **không gửi lên happy-server**. **UNCERTAIN (verdict):** client_id này confirmed là OAuth app chính thức của Anthropic (xuất hiện trong nhiều issue `anthropics/claude-code`), NHƯNG happy chỉ xin scope hẹp `user:inference` trong khi `claude` CLI chính thức xin scope rộng hơn (`org:create_api_key+user:profile+user:inference+user:sessions:claude_code+...`). Việc `user:inference` đơn lẻ có cấp đủ quota Pro/Max hay chỉ API-billed thì **không xác nhận được** từ source — Anthropic không public mapping scope→access. Đừng coi đây là chắc chắn.

**Client tech:** `happy-app` = Expo `~55.0.8` + React Native `0.83.1` (iOS/Android/web) **+ Tauri `~2.9`** cho desktop (`src-tauri/Cargo.toml`). `codium` = Electron riêng (`electron.vite.config.ts`, có `TerminalHost.tsx`/`TerminalPane.tsx`). Hai desktop target cùng tồn tại — corpus cũ chỉ nhắc Electron, corpus mới chỉ nhắc Tauri; **cả hai đều đúng, không mâu thuẫn**.

**Session model:** session ID lưu server (cuid2) gắn với Machine; resume bằng `--resume <uuid>` truyền cho Claude/SDK; `sessionScanner` dedup theo `uuid` để replay-safe khi reconnect/multi-device; multiplexing nhiều session/máy qua daemon control server (`127.0.0.1`); push qua Expo Push API (`expo-server-sdk`).

---

### 2. happier-dev/happier móc vào kiểu gì + khác happy chỗ nào

**Fork hay độc lập?** Honest: README nói "originally started as contributors to Happy… started building for ourselves" — về mặt sản phẩm là **reimplementation độc lập** với monorepo riêng (`apps/cli`, `apps/server`, `apps/ui`, `packages/protocol`, `packages/agents`), không thấy shared git history. Nhưng về code-shape rõ ràng **bắt nguồn từ happy** (cùng cấu trúc `claudeLocal`/`claudeRemote`/`startHookServer`/`generateHookSettings`, cùng pattern keypair-auth + Socket.IO `/v1/updates`). Coi là "fork tinh thần đã diverge xa", không phải clone.

**Hook mechanism — giống happy nhưng tiến hóa:**
- LOCAL: `apps/cli/src/backends/claude/claudeLocal.ts:427` cũng `stdio: ['inherit','inherit','inherit','pipe']` — PTY passthrough thật, fd 3 cho thinking-state.
- REMOTE: `claudeRemoteAgentSdk.ts` import `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` (`query as agentSdkQuery`), spawn `claude --output-format stream-json --input-format stream-json`; có `Query` class tự bọc, xử lý `control_request`/`control_response` cho permission callback, và **fallback** sang codepath `--print` legacy khi lỗi auth/missing-exec.

**Ba điểm khác happy quan trọng:**
1. **`--plugin-dir` thay cho `--settings`** (production-hardened lesson). `generateHookSettings.ts` comment nêu rõ: `--settings` là last-write-wins, một PATH wrapper (vd cmux) prepend `--settings` của nó sẽ **âm thầm nuốt** hook block của happier. → happier để non-hook config (`permissions.allow`) trong `--settings`, còn hooks chuyển sang `--plugin-dir` (additive, nhiều invocation cộng dồn). `--plugin-dir` **confirmed** là flag chính thức, stable (`code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins`). happy hiện vẫn ở `--settings`.
2. **Schema hook không ổn định → parse phòng thủ** (verdict: confirmed). `startHookServer.ts` của happier parse CẢ snake_case (`session_id`, `transcript_path`) LẪN camelCase (`sessionId`, `transcriptPath`); happy chỉ có `session_id`/`sessionId` và `transcript_path` (thiếu `transcriptPath`). Delta này là bằng chứng schema đã đổi giữa các version Claude Code. happier thêm cả `PermissionRequest` hook (`/hook/permission-request`) để mobile approve/deny tool — happy không có.
3. **Multi-backend qua ACP**: ngoài Claude, happier support codex/opencode/gemini/qwen/copilot/cursor… qua `AcpBackend.ts` (`@agentclientprotocol/sdk`, NDJSON stdin/stdout). Auth Claude reuse `~/.claude/.credentials.json` + `~/.claude/.claude.json`.

**Transport/auth/client:** Socket.IO `/v1/updates` tới `api.happier.dev` (default, override `HAPPIER_SERVER_URL`), self-host Docker (Postgres/SQLite/MySQL), nhiều auth backend enterprise (GitHub org/team gating, OIDC, mTLS, keyless). E2E TweetNaCl X25519+XSalsa20-Poly1305 (`boxBundle.ts`), zero-knowledge ở crypto layer (chưa audit toàn server-side codepath — verdict ghi nhận honest). Claude auth tách hẳn relay auth: `isolateClaudeRuntimeAuthEnv.ts` lọc chọn lọc `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`/`CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`/`CLAUDE_CODE_SETUP_TOKEN` forward vào subprocess theo "connected service" profile. Client: Expo 54 + RN 0.81.5 + Tauri 2.8, libsodium on-device, tmux cho persistent headless session, push "Claude ready" kèm preview.

---

### 3. Bảng so sánh hai dự án

| Tiêu chí | slopus/happy | happier-dev/happier |
|---|---|---|
| **Hook — local** | `claude` binary, `stdio inherit` (full TUI), fd 3 thinking, sessionScanner đọc JSONL | Giống hệt (`claudeLocal.ts:427`), cùng pattern |
| **Hook — remote** | `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk ^0.2.96` (`sdkQuery`) → stream-json *(corpus cũ nói "không SDK" → SAI, đã đính chính)* | `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` + fallback `--print`; ACP cho non-Claude |
| **Cách inject hook** | `--settings` (file SessionStart) + `--mcp-config` | **`--plugin-dir` (additive)** + `--settings` cho non-hook; tránh cmux nuốt hook |
| **Hook payload parse** | chỉ snake_case + một phần camelCase | snake_case **và** camelCase đầy đủ (phòng thủ schema drift) |
| **Permission từ mobile** | (chủ yếu MCP/RPC) | `PermissionRequest` hook riêng |
| **Transport/relay** | Socket.IO `api.cluster-fluster.com`, **bắt buộc, không P2P** | Socket.IO `api.happier.dev`, không P2P, có Tailscale Serve để self-host |
| **Encryption** | E2E NaCl secretbox / AES-256-GCM dataKey; variant chốt lúc auth (không per-session push) | E2E TweetNaCl X25519+XSalsa20-Poly1305, zero-knowledge ở crypto layer |
| **Auth relay** | keypair NaCl challenge → JWT | keypair challenge + OAuth GitHub/OIDC/mTLS (enterprise) |
| **Auth Claude** | PKCE `user:inference` (scope-to-access *uncertain*) | reuse `~/.claude` creds + isolate env per profile |
| **Client tech** | Expo 55 + RN 0.83.1 + **Tauri ~2.9**; **codium = Electron** riêng | Expo 54 + RN 0.81.5 + **Tauri 2.8** |
| **Self-host** | Có (Docker, `HAPPY_SERVER_URL`), nhưng prod cluster mặc định | Có, mạnh hơn (Postgres/SQLite/MySQL, multi auth backend, ProxmoxVE docs) |
| **Multi-agent** | Chỉ Claude (+ codex/gemini subcommand) | Catalog rộng qua ACP (codex/opencode/gemini/qwen/copilot/cursor…) |

---

### 4. BÀI HỌC cho dự án của ta (PTY-over-NetBird + libghostty)

**Nên MƯỢN:**

1. **SessionStart hook để lấy session ID — và dùng `--plugin-dir`, KHÔNG dùng `--settings`.** Đây là bài học production-hardened đắt giá nhất của happier: PATH wrapper (cmux…) sẽ âm thầm nuốt `--settings`. Dù ta relay byte PTY, ta vẫn cần biết Claude session UUID + transcript path để map resume/handoff — file-watching JSONL có race khi nhiều process chạy cùng project dir; hook cho guarantee 1:1 process→session. Parse **cả** `session_id`/`sessionId` **và** `transcript_path`/`transcriptPath` (schema đã đổi giữa version).
2. **Tách bạch hai tầng credential** (cả hai dự án đều làm): auth transport của ta (NetBird keypair / setup-token) **độc lập hoàn toàn** với auth Claude. Forward `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` / `CLAUDE_CODE_SETUP_TOKEN` vào subprocess env, dùng pattern `isolateClaudeRuntimeAuthEnv` (lọc chọn lọc theo profile). **Cảnh báo honest:** đừng copy nguyên scope `user:inference` của happy mà không kiểm chứng — verdict để mở việc scope đó có cấp quota Pro/Max hay không. An toàn nhất: defer cho `claude` đã login sẵn (reuse `~/.claude/.credentials.json`) thay vì tự chạy PKCE.
3. **Session resume replay-safe** bằng dedup theo uuid (happy `sessionScanner`) + monotonic `seq` server-side. Sau khi NetBird reconnect, client cần biết đã miss message nào — một `seq` đơn giản giải quyết.
4. **Auth bằng keypair challenge-response → token** (không password/email) cho machine registration — sạch, không có credential để phish. Hợp với setup-token flow của ta.
5. **Push notification có presence-suppression + preview "Claude ready"**. Route qua một dịch vụ để suppress khi session đang được xem (tránh spam). Trong mô hình P2P của ta, host process tự trigger APNs/FCM trực tiếp — cân nhắc APNs/FCM thẳng thay vì Expo Push (Expo đưa thêm infra của bên thứ ba vào delivery path, kém phù hợp cho tool privacy-focused).
6. **tmux làm persistence layer** cho headless session (happier dùng `startHappyHeadlessInTmux.ts`). Ta đặt PTY trong tmux pane → libghostty client attach/detach độc lập, sống sót qua SSH disconnect.
7. **E2E encrypt ở application layer** nếu sau này ta thêm signaling/history server: dù NetBird đã là WireGuard E2E cho byte path, mọi store metadata/transcript nên wrap key per-session (AES-256-GCM + wrapped DEK) để server không bao giờ thấy plaintext.

**Ta làm KHÁC / TỐT HƠN:**

1. **Full TUI fidelity qua libghostty.** Cả hai dự án ở local mode chỉ inherit stdin/stdout ra terminal host và **không bao giờ stream byte TUI** xuống mobile — mobile chỉ thấy structured JSON events. Ta relay raw PTY (`TERM=xterm-ghostty`) → giữ nguyên màu/cursor/selection/compose-box, đúng như Claude render. Đây là khác biệt bản chất.
2. **P2P, không relay SPOF.** Cả happy lẫn happier đều chết nếu relay (`api.cluster-fluster.com` / `api.happier.dev`) sập. NetBird P2P loại bỏ relay khỏi byte path → latency gần-zero (không +50–200ms RTT relay), loại một trust boundary.
3. **Một codepath PTY duy nhất**, tránh combinatorial surface của hai launcher (local TUI + remote SDK) mà cả hai dự án phải maintain.

**Pitfall họ gặp — ta tránh:**

1. **`stdin O_NONBLOCK` (happy issue #301).** `claudeLocal.ts:191` phải `setBlocking(true)` để xóa `O_NONBLOCK` Node/libuv để lại, nếu không TUI bị echo garbled / cursor nhân đôi. Ta **sẽ gặp** y hệt khi inherit/relay PTY → set blocking trước khi spawn.
2. **`CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT`.** SDK headless mặc định set entrypoint `sdk-cli`/`sdk-ts` → picker `claude --resume` lọc bỏ các session đó. Set `CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT=remote_mobile` (giá trị non-SDK) khi spawn headless để user vẫn resume được từ terminal.
3. **Duplicate prompt forwarding.** Khi vừa có compose-box (app gửi) vừa có PTY (user gõ), cùng một prompt lọt vào JSONL hai lần. Cả hai dự án giữ dedup ring buffer (text + timestamp). Tool của ta phơi bày *cả* compose-box *và* PTY đồng thời → phải plan dedup này.
4. **`--settings` non-composable** (đã nêu) và **schema hook drift** (snake/camel) — dùng `--plugin-dir` + parse phòng thủ.
5. **Orphan sidechain buffering** khi `Task` tool spawn subagent (message subagent đến trước parent tool-call) — `sessionProtocolMapper` của họ có logic buffer phức tạp. Trong PTY mode việc này **vô hình** (ta relay byte, không structured event) → ta tránh được, nhưng nếu ta layer structured event lên trên cho iOS thì sẽ phải đối mặt.

**Có nên đổi kiến trúc không? (honest)**

Không cần đổi trục chính, NHƯNG nên **lai một phần**:

- **Desktop (macOS libghostty): giữ nguyên PTY-over-NetBird** — đây là điểm mạnh, full fidelity, đúng hướng.
- **iOS/iPadOS: cân nhắc layer structured-event stream lên trên PTY** thay vì bắt user vừa pinch-zoom terminal ANSI trên màn nhỏ. Cả happy lẫn happier đều kết luận mobile cần native UI (text/tool-call-start/end/turn) chứ không phải raw TUI. Ta có thể emit synthetic event từ terminal parser của libghostty (ta đã có byte stream — parse thêm event là incremental), giữ raw-PTY làm fallback "xem terminal thật". Đây là điểm mở (open question của corpus): liệu user có thực sự muốn raw TUI trên mobile — đáng làm prototype A/B.
- **Vẫn cần một dịch vụ coordination nhẹ** (device discovery, push, session metadata persistence) dù transport là P2P — NetBird lo byte path nhưng push notification và "machine offline thì queue prompt" cần một control plane. Đừng ảo tưởng P2P xóa được 100% server; chỉ xóa được relay khỏi *byte path*.

**Nguồn đã đọc trực tiếp (clone lại để verify):** `slopus/happy` `packages/happy-cli/src/claude/sdk/query.ts:6,80`, `package.json` (`@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk@^0.2.96`), `claudeLocal.ts:191,316`, `generateHookSettings.ts:4,34`, `happy-app/src-tauri/Cargo.toml` (tauri ~2.9, expo ~55.0.8, RN 0.83.1), `packages/codium/electron.vite.config.ts`. Các nguồn còn lại theo corpus verified: `docs/encryption.md`, `configuration.ts:59`, `pushNotifications.ts`, `authenticateClaude.ts`; happier `apps/cli/src/backends/claude/{claudeLocal.ts:427, remote/claudeRemoteAgentSdk.ts, utils/{startHookServer.ts, generateHookSettings.ts}}`, `packages/protocol/src/crypto/boxBundle.ts`, `packages/agents/src/{auth.ts, localCli.ts}`, README.

corpus.summary: slopus/happy is a three-component system: a Node.js CLI daemon (happy-cli) that wraps the claude binary in TWO modes (interactive PTY for local use, headless --output-format stream-json SDK for remote use), a Fastify/Socket.IO relay server (happy-server), and a React Native/Expo mobile + Electron (codium) desktop client. The CLI encrypts all content end-to-end (NaCl secretbox / AES-256-GCM) before sending to the relay. The relay is a required intermediary — there is no P2P path. Auth to Claude is via the user's own Claude.ai OAuth tokens (PKCE); auth to the happy-server relay uses a local NaCl keypair challenge-response that produces a relay JWT.
corpus.hook_mechanism:
Two launcher paths, both spawning the claude binary — not the Anthropic Agent SDK library directly:

REMOTE MODE (primary for mobile use):
query() in happy-cli/src/claude/sdk/query.ts spawns: `node <claude_remote_launcher.cjs> --output-format stream-json --verbose --input-format stream-json --permission-prompt-tool stdio --settings <hook-settings-path> [...]`
The launcher wraps global `claude` (or bundled @anthropic-ai/claude-code/cli.js) with fetch-intercepting for thinking-state tracking on fd 3. Messages flow as JSONL on stdout; user messages are streamed to stdin as stream-json. This is the headless SDK / `claude -p --output-format stream-json` approach (option b in the prompt).

LOCAL MODE (interactive terminal):
claudeLocal() in happy-cli/src/claude/claudeLocal.ts spawns `node <claude_local_launcher.cjs> [args]` with stdio: ['inherit','inherit','inherit','pipe']. This passes the TTY through — the full TUI is presented to the user on the host. fd 3 carries fetch-start/fetch-end JSON events to detect when Claude is "thinking". This is PTY-relay (option a), but the byte stream is NOT forwarded to mobile — the mobile client only sees structured session events ingested by sessionScanner from the JSONL files Claude writes to disk.

HOOK MECHANISM for session ID tracking:
startHookServer() (happy-cli/src/claude/utils/startHookServer.ts) starts an HTTP server on a random loopback port. generateHookSettingsFile() (happy-cli/src/claude/utils/generateHookSettings.ts) writes a temporary ~/.happy/tmp/hooks/session-hook-<pid>.json containing: `{ "hooks": { "SessionStart": [{ "matcher": "*", "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "node session_hook_forwarder.cjs <port>" }] }] } }`. Claude is spawned with `--settings <that-file>`. On every SessionStart event Claude pipes JSON to the forwarder's stdin; session_hook_forwarder.cjs (happy-cli/scripts/session_hook_forwarder.cjs) HTTP-POSTs it to 127.0.0.1:<port>/hook/session-start. This is how the CLI learns the Claude session ID without parsing raw output.

MCP server: startHappyServer() starts an MCP HTTP server exposing tool names injected via --allowedTools mcp__happy__* and --mcp-config to every claude spawn. This gives Claude tools to interact with the happy relay layer.
corpus.architecture:
Host side (user's machine):
- happy-cli (Node.js/TypeScript) — the central daemon. Runs as a background launchd service on macOS (src/daemon/mac/install.ts). Exposes a local HTTP control server on 127.0.0.1 (src/daemon/controlServer.ts) for IPC. Spawns Claude sessions as child processes. Maintains a long-lived Socket.IO connection to happy-server.
- Two spawn modes: interactive (local, PTY passthrough for terminal users) and headless (remote, stream-json for mobile-driven sessions).
- Session scanner (src/claude/utils/sessionScanner.ts) watches Claude's JSONL session files on disk, deduplicates by uuid, and forwards structured messages to the relay.
- Hook server + forwarder receive Claude's SessionStart hook to track session IDs.
- MCP happy server exposes tools back to Claude.

Relay server (cloud, required):
- happy-server (Node.js Fastify + Socket.IO + Prisma + Postgres + optional Redis for multi-replica). Deployed on slopus's infrastructure (api.cluster-fluster.com implied by push token URL).
- Three Socket.IO connection scopes: user-scoped (app clients), session-scoped (one CLI per session), machine-scoped (one daemon per machine).
- RPC over Socket.IO: mobile → server → daemon → spawn-session / bash / ripgrep / difftastic.
- The server is intentionally content-blind: all message bodies are opaque base64 blobs.
- Redis streams adapter for multi-replica fan-out.
- Self-hostable (HAPPY_SERVER_URL env var, Dockerfile provided).

Client apps:
- happy-app: React Native 0.83.1 + Expo 55 (iOS, Android, web). App Store + Google Play + web app.
- codium: Electron app (packages/codium, electron.vite.config.ts) — desktop companion with embedded terminal (TerminalHost.tsx, TerminalPane.tsx) and full chat UI.
- Both share happy-wire protocol types (@slopus/happy-wire package).
corpus.transport_auth:
Transport:
- CLI ↔ server: Socket.IO over HTTPS/WSS to happy-server. HTTP REST for session/machine CRUD. No P2P. The relay is always in the path.
- Server uses Redis streams adapter when REDIS_URL is set for multi-instance scaling.
- Push notifications: Expo Push API (expo-server-sdk in happy-cli/src/api/pushNotifications.ts).
- Voice: ElevenLabs Realtime (paid feature).

Encryption (E2E, verified):
- All user content is encrypted client-side before leaving the machine. Server stores opaque base64 blobs; it cannot read messages, metadata, or agent state.
- Two variants (docs/encryption.md): legacy = NaCl secretbox (XSalsa20-Poly1305, 32-byte shared secret); dataKey = AES-256-GCM with per-session key wrapped via tweetnacl.box ephemeral keypair.
- Encrypted fields: session messages, session metadata, agent state, machine metadata, daemon state, artifacts, KV store values, access keys.
- Server-side encryption (NOT E2E): GitHub OAuth tokens and vendor API keys encrypted at rest using HANDY_MASTER_SECRET KeyTree.

Auth model (two independent layers):
1. Happy relay auth: Client generates a NaCl keypair stored in ~/.happy/access.key. Auth flow: sign a challenge with private key → POST /v1/auth {publicKey, challenge, signature} → server verifies (tweetnacl), upserts Account by publicKey, returns a JWT (signed with HANDY_MASTER_SECRET). JWT used as Bearer token on all subsequent requests. Happy account CUID is the user's identity. No password or email required.

2. Claude / Anthropic auth: happy-cli/src/commands/connect/authenticateClaude.ts implements a full PKCE OAuth2 flow against claude.ai/oauth/authorize using client_id=9d1c250a-e61b-44d9-88ed-5944d1962f5e and scope=user:inference. Tokens exchanged at console.anthropic.com/v1/oauth/token. The resulting access_token is used directly when spawning claude (set in env / passed to Claude Code's own auth layer). This reuses the user's existing Claude subscription — no separate API key needed for Claude access. The token is stored locally and passed to the claude process environment; it is NOT sent to happy-server.
corpus.client_tech:
Mobile: React Native 0.83.1 + Expo 55 (packages/happy-app). Targets iOS, Android, and web via expo start --web. Uses expo-server-sdk on the CLI side for push notifications. ElevenLabs Realtime for voice. LiveKit patched (patches/fix-livekit-room-reuse.cjs).

Desktop: Electron app via electron-vite (packages/codium). React-based UI with TerminalHost/TerminalPane components (full terminal widget embedded in the desktop app). Likely uses xterm.js or similar for terminal rendering (packages/codium/sources/app/components/terminal/).

Shared: @slopus/happy-wire for protocol type definitions. PGlite + Prisma for client-side persistence (patches/fix-pglite-prisma-bytes.cjs). Preact patches also present (patches/fix-pierre-trees-preact-hooks.cjs, patches/expose-pierre-diffs-style.cjs).

points.detail: All CLI↔mobile traffic flows through the Socket.IO relay. The relay is self-hostable (HAPPY_SERVER_URL env var, Dockerfile.server present in happy repo and separate happy-server repo), but a central instance runs at api.cluster-fluster.com (push notifications URL). NetBird/WireGuard is not used.

points.claim: Session resume works across devices via server-stored JSONL session IDs; sessionScanner deduplicates replays
points.detail: Claude writes JSONL session files to disk keyed by session UUID. sessionScanner tracks processedMessageKeys (by uuid field) so restarts and multi-device reconnects don't replay already-sent messages. onNewSession() guards prevent re-activating already-finished sessions.

points.claim: Push notifications use Expo Push API (not APNs/FCM directly)

This means the Expo notification infrastructure is in the delivery path, which requires the app to be built with Expo's managed workflow or bare workflow with expo-notifications.

points.claim: Desktop client (codium) is Electron with an embedded terminal widget, not a PTY-over-relay
points.detail: packages/codium uses electron-vite (electron.vite.config.ts) and has TerminalHost.tsx and TerminalPane.tsx components in the UI layer. This Electron app runs on the same machine as the CLI daemon and can interact directly.

lessons: Do not relay raw PTY bytes to mobile. Happy proves structured events (turn-start, text, tool-call-start/end, turn-end) give a richer mobile UX than raw ANSI. Our libghostty approach (PTY byte relay) is better for desktop fidelity but for mobile we should layer a structured event stream on top of the PTY bytes — emit synthetic events from the terminal parser.

Use Claude Code's native SessionStart hook (--settings file with hooks.SessionStart) to track session IDs without parsing stdout. This is clean, official, and survives Claude updates. We should adopt the same pattern for our daemon layer.

The two-mode design (local=interactive PTY, remote=headless SDK) is elegant: the host machine can still use Claude Code normally in the terminal while mobile observes. Our tool should distinguish 'supervised' (user at keyboard, mobile observes) from 'autonomous' (mobile drives, stream-json) modes rather than forcing one model.

Encrypt everything client-side before it touches the relay. Happy's approach (NaCl secretbox + AES-256-GCM with per-session wrapped keys) means the relay operator cannot read user data. For our NetBird P2P approach the relay is eliminated entirely, which is strictly better — but if we ever add a signaling server or session-persistence server, apply the same E2E encryption pattern.

Relay auth via local keypair (NaCl challenge-response → JWT) is cleaner than password/email. No credentials to phish. We should adopt the same keypair-based auth for our setup-token / machine registration flow.

Claude's PKCE OAuth flow (client_id=9d1c250a-e61b-44d9-88ed-5944d1962f5e, scope=user:inference, claude.ai/oauth/authorize) is the correct way to reuse the user's subscription without an API key. We should implement the same flow — or defer to the already-running claude process's auth session — rather than prompting for an API key.

The daemon model (long-lived background process, launchd on macOS, local HTTP control server for IPC) is the right architecture for always-available remote access. Our host-side component should follow this: install a launchd/systemd unit, expose a local IPC socket, and report machine state to the signaling layer.

sessionScanner's JSONL deduplication by uuid is a simple, robust solution for replay-safe session resume across restarts and multi-device reconnects. We should implement equivalent deduplication in our PTY session store.

Expo Push API is the fastest path to APNs+FCM with one SDK, but it adds Expo's infrastructure to the delivery path. For a privacy-focused tool, consider direct APNs/FCM or use Expo's self-hosted push gateway.

The codium Electron app shows that desktop and mobile need different UX paradigms even for the same backend. Our libghostty client on macOS can afford full terminal fidelity; the iOS/iPadOS client should lean on the structured event stream for a native-feeling UI rather than embedding a terminal emulator.

The client_id 9d1c250a-e61b-44d9-88ed-5944d1962f5e IS confirmed as the official Anthropic/Claude Code OAuth app — it appears verbatim in multiple official Claude Code bug reports filed against github.com/anthropics/claude-code (issues #36215, #954, #39445) with the exact same PKCE flow against claude.ai/oauth/authorize and console.anthropic.com. The claim that 'scope=user:inference still grants subscription-level inference access' is therefore plausible but unconfirmed; it may grant only API-billed access, not Max/Pro subscription access.

## FULL RESEARCH FINDINGS

### PROJECT 1: slopus/happy

**Repository structure:** A pnpm monorepo with packages: `happy-cli`, `happy-server`, `happy-app`, `happy-agent`, `happy-wire`, `codium`. happier-dev/happier is a direct fork (docs nearly identical, packages renamed `apps/cli`, `apps/server`, `apps/ui`).

---

#### Architecture

**Host-side component:** `packages/happy-cli` — a Node.js CLI daemon (`happy`) that runs on the developer's machine. On startup it:
- Parses subcommands (default, `auth`, `connect`, `codex`, `gemini`, `doctor`) in [`src/index.ts`](https://github.com/slopus/happy/blob/main/packages/happy-cli/src/index.ts)
- Registers the machine with the server (`POST /v1/machines`) and starts a background **daemon process**
- The daemon runs a local control HTTP server on `127.0.0.1:<random-port>` (documented in [`daemon/controlServer.ts`](https://github.com/slopus/happy/blob/main/packages/happy-cli/src/daemon/controlServer.ts)) exposing `/spawn-session`, `/list`, `/stop-session`, `/stop`

**Client app:** `packages/happy-app` — React Native / Expo app with a **Tauri v2** desktop wrapper (`src-tauri/Cargo.toml` declares `tauri = "~2.9"`). It builds for iOS, Android, Web, and macOS desktop. Push notifications use Expo's push infrastructure.

**Relay/server:** `packages/happy-server` — a Node.js Fastify + Socket.IO server. **Not self-hostable by default** — the production instance is at `https://api.cluster-fluster.com` (hardcoded in `configuration.ts`). But the server is open-source and the env vars `HAPPY_SERVER_URL`/`HAPPY_WEBAPP_URL` allow self-hosting. Stack: Fastify, Socket.IO on `/v1/updates`, Postgres (Prisma), Redis, S3/MinIO.

---

#### How it hooks Claude Code — TWO modes

**Mode A: Local launcher (claudeLocal)**

Documented in [`claudeLocalLauncher.ts`](https://github.com/slopus/happy/blob/main/packages/happy-cli/src/claude/claudeLocalLauncher.ts) and [`claudeLocal.ts`](https://github.com/slopus/happy/blob/main/packages/happy-cli/src/claude/claudeLocal.ts):

The CLI spawns the `claude` binary as a **child process** using `cross-spawn`. The critical path:
1. `claudeCliPath` points to `scripts/claude_local_launcher.cjs` — a wrapper script in the repo
2. The process is invoked as `node claude_local_launcher.cjs --session-id <uuid> --append-system-prompt ... --mcp-config ... --settings <hookSettingsPath>`
3. The `--settings` flag passes a **temporary Claude settings JSON** file (in `~/.happy/tmp/hooks/session-hook-<pid>.json`) generated by [`generateHookSettingsFile()`](https://github.com/slopus/happy/blob/main/packages/happy-cli/src/claude/utils/generateHookSettings.ts):
   ```json
   {
     "hooks": {
       "SessionStart": [{
         "matcher": "*",
         "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "node session_hook_forwarder.cjs <port>" }]
       }]
     }
   }
   ```
4. This uses **Claude Code's native hooks feature** to fire a `SessionStart` event. The `session_hook_forwarder.cjs` script receives the session data on stdin and HTTP POSTs it to a local hook server at `127.0.0.1:<port>/hook/session-start` (started by [`startHookServer.ts`](https://github.com/slopus/happy/blob/main/packages/happy-cli/src/claude/utils/startHookServer.ts))
5. The hook server reads the `session_id` and notifies the `SessionScanner`, which file-watches Claude's JSONL transcript files for new messages

This is **approach (a)** (spawns `claude` CLI in a child process) combined with **approach (c)** (uses Claude Code hooks via `--settings`). Claude Code's interactive TUI runs normally; the wrapper ingests the JSONL output, not the TUI bytes.

**Mode B: Remote launcher (claudeRemote / SDK)**

When the user sends a message from mobile, the session switches to "remote mode" ([`claudeRemoteLauncher.ts`](https://github.com/slopus/happy/blob/main/packages/happy-cli/src/claude/claudeRemoteLauncher.ts)):
- Uses the **Claude Code SDK** (`@anthropic-ai/sdk` via the `query()` wrapper in `src/claude/sdk/query.ts`) — **headless**, no TUI
- The SDK stream is converted by `SDKToLogConverter` and passed to the same `sessionProtocolMapper` that maps Claude messages to the unified session protocol
- Incoming messages from mobile arrive via the relay server (Socket.IO RPC `message` event) and are fed into the SDK as the next user turn

This is **approach (b)** (headless `claude` SDK / `claude -p --output-format stream-json` semantics).

---

#### Transport

**Host ↔ relay:** Socket.IO WebSocket at `wss://api.cluster-fluster.com/v1/updates`. The handshake carries `{ token, clientType: "session-scoped"|"machine-scoped", sessionId?, machineId? }`. HTTP for CRUD. All payloads are **end-to-end encrypted** client-side before reaching the relay — the server sees only opaque base64 blobs.

**Encryption:** Two variants (from [`encryption.md`](https://github.com/slopus/happy/blob/main/docs/encryption.md)):
- Legacy: `tweetnacl.secretbox` (XSalsa20-Poly1305, 32-byte shared secret)
- DataKey: AES-256-GCM with per-session key, key-wrapped via `tweetnacl.box`

**Not P2P.** All traffic flows through the managed relay at `api.cluster-fluster.com`. The relay is a standard cloud server — no WireGuard/NetBird.

**Push notifications:** Expo Push API (`expo-server-sdk`). For session events, routed via the server at `POST /v1/sessions/{sessionId}/push-event` so the server can apply presence-based suppression. The server fetches registered push tokens from `GET /v1/push-tokens`.

---

#### Auth

The system uses **its own auth** entirely separate from Claude Code's OAuth:
- CLI generates an Ed25519 keypair (`access.key`)
- Auth: `POST /v1/auth` with `{ publicKey, challenge, signature }` → returns a Bearer token
- The server upserts an `Account` keyed on the public key
- The Bearer token is used for all subsequent HTTP + Socket.IO calls
- Claude Code auth (Anthropic subscription/API key) is handled separately — Claude Code uses its own `~/.claude` credentials. The `connect` subcommand stores vendor API keys (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini) as server-side encrypted tokens

---

#### Session model

- Each session has a stable server-side ID (cuid2), associated with a `Machine` (the host daemon)
- Resume: the daemon tracks `sessionId` locally; on reconnect, the CLI re-attaches by passing `--resume <sessionId>` to the `claude` binary or the SDK's `sessionId` field
- Multiple sessions: the daemon manages a map of child session processes; sessions can be spawned remotely via RPC `spawn-session`
- Multiple devices: all devices connect to the same relay server under the same account Bearer token; the monotonic `seq` per user ensures consistent ordering
- Session multiplexing: supported — multiple sessions can be active simultaneously on one machine, tracked by the daemon's control server

---

### PROJECT 2: happier-dev/happier

happier-dev/happier is a **direct fork** of slopus/happy with renames and extensions:
- `packages/` → `apps/` for server/cli/ui
- `~/.happy` → `~/.happier`, `HAPPY_*` env vars → `HAPPIER_*`
- Default server: `https://api.happier.dev` (from [`apps/cli/src/configuration.ts:1028`](https://github.com/happier-dev/happier/blob/main/apps/cli/src/configuration.ts))
- The server deploy manifests still reference `files.cluster-fluster.com/happy` for static assets, indicating shared infrastructure

**Key architectural difference:** happier-dev extends the system to support **multiple AI CLI backends** beyond Claude Code. The `packages/agents` package defines a catalog of providers:
- `claude`, `codex`, `opencode`, `gemini`, `auggie`, `qwen`, `kimi`, `kiro`, `kilo`, `copilot`, `cursor` (from [`providerCliRuntime.ts`](https://github.com/happier-dev/happier/blob/main/packages/agents/src/providers/providerCliRuntime.ts))

**How it hooks Claude Code (happier-specific):**

happier uses the **ACP (Agent Client Protocol) pattern** via `@agentclientprotocol/sdk`. The `AcpBackend` class ([`apps/cli/src/agent/acp/AcpBackend.ts`](https://github.com/happier-dev/happier/blob/main/apps/cli/src/agent/acp/AcpBackend.ts)):
- Spawns the agent CLI (e.g. `claude`) as a child process with `cross-spawn` via `buildAcpSpawnSpec`
- Communicates over **stdin/stdout pipes** using the ACP protocol (NDJSON stream)
- The `AgentLocalCliConfig` for Claude (`packages/agents/src/localCli.ts:49`) specifies `binaryName: 'claude'`, `loginLaunch.initialInput: '/login\r'`
- This is approach **(b)** — headless API via ACP protocol, not PTY relay

For Claude specifically, auth credential detection checks `~/.claude/.credentials.json` and `~/.claude/.claude.json` (from [`auth.ts`](https://github.com/happier-dev/happier/blob/main/packages/agents/src/auth.ts)) — reusing the existing Claude Code OAuth session.

The `packages/relay-server` in happier is **not a network relay** — it's a package for computing release asset checksums and minisign verification (files: `checksums.mjs`, `minisign.mjs`, `releaseAssets.mjs`). Network relay is still the same Socket.IO relay server.

**Client:** Same Expo/React Native + Tauri v2 architecture. `apps/ui/src-tauri/Cargo.toml` declares `tauri = "2.8.2"`.

---

### COMPARISON TO YOUR APPROACH (PTY-over-NetBird + libghostty)

| Dimension | slopus/happy & happier | Your approach |
|---|---|---|
| **How Claude is launched** | Spawns `claude` binary as child process (local TUI mode) or SDK headless (remote mode) | PTY — full interactive TUI byte stream |
| **What the client sees** | Normalized structured messages (session protocol events: `text`, `tool-call-start`, `tool-call-end`, `turn-start`, `turn-end`) — Claude's TUI is never transmitted | Raw terminal bytes from Claude's actual TUI via libghostty |
| **Transport** | Managed relay (Socket.IO, cloud-hosted at `api.cluster-fluster.com` / `api.happier.dev`) | P2P WireGuard (NetBird), no relay server |
| **Encryption** | E2EE client-side (AES-256-GCM / NaCl), relay is blind | WireGuard is E2EE; libghostty terminal state is local |
| **Relay dependency** | Required for all connectivity — single point of failure | None; direct P2P — survives relay outages |
| **Latency** | Relay RTT adds ~50–200ms for interactive feel | Near-zero relay latency (WireGuard is direct) |
| **Auth** | Own public-key auth system (Ed25519 challenge) separate from Claude Code subscription | Reuses Claude Code OAuth/subscription session directly |
| **Claude version fidelity** | Must maintain a mapping layer (session protocol) that can break when Claude adds new tool types | Zero — the TUI stream is what Claude produces, verbatim |
| **Mobile fidelity** | Purpose-built mobile-native UI (custom rendering of structured events) | libghostty renders terminal directly — "real" TUI on mobile |
| **Compose box** | Messages sent as RPC over relay → fed to SDK/subprocess as next turn | Compose box injects into PTY stdin |
| **Offline resilience** | Sessions persist on relay server; clients can catch up on reconnect via monotonic `seq` | Depends on NetBird connectivity; no persistent relay buffer |
| **Multi-device** | All devices see same session stream via relay fan-out | Possible via NetBird multi-peer; needs design |

---

### LESSONS TO EXTRACT

**Borrow:**
1. **Session hook mechanism** (approach c): The `--settings` file trick to inject a `SessionStart` hook is clean and non-invasive. For local mode, this is the only reliable way to know the Claude session ID (especially after `/compact` forks). Adopt this even in PTY mode to track session changes without parsing TUI bytes.
2. **Monotonic `seq` for session resume**: Both projects use a per-user monotonic counter for ordering. After a NetBird reconnect, a client needs to know which messages it missed. A simple server-side seq solves this.
3. **`daemon.state.json` for IPC**: The pattern of writing daemon PID + control port to a JSON file for local IPC is simple and reliable across OS restarts.
4. **Presence suppression for push notifications**: Routing push through the server so it can suppress if the session is actively viewed avoids notification spam during active sessions.
5. **Per-session E2EE key wrapping** (AES-256-GCM with wrapped DEK): Even though your relay is P2P WireGuard, if you ever add a session history store, this model ensures the server (or future relay) never sees plaintext.

**Do differently / better:**
1. **PTY = full TUI fidelity**: Neither project transmits the actual terminal. You get `TERM=xterm-ghostty` + full Warp-style compose box — a fundamentally richer experience than their structured-message client.
2. **No relay dependency**: NetBird P2P means no `api.cluster-fluster.com` SPOF. Both projects' mobile clients are dead if their relay is down.
3. **Auth reuse**: Both projects require users to auth with a separate system (their own Ed25519 keys + server Bearer token) *in addition to* Claude Code's own auth. Your setup-token approach can reuse Claude Code's OAuth session directly.

**Pitfalls they hit (avoid):**
1. **Orphan sidechain buffering**: When Claude's `Task` tool spawns subagents, the subagent messages can arrive before the parent tool call. Their `sessionProtocolMapper` has complex buffering logic for this. In PTY mode this is invisible — you transmit bytes, not structured events.
2. **SDK vs local mode split**: Maintaining two launchers (TUI child process for local, SDK headless for remote) creates a combinatorial surface of edge cases. Your single PTY path avoids this duality entirely.
3. **JSONL file polling race conditions**: The local launcher watches Claude's JSONL transcript files with a `SessionScanner`. This is fragile when multiple Happy processes run against the same project directory. Their hook-based approach (`--settings` with `SessionStart`) was added to fix this. In PTY mode, stdout *is* the stream — no file watching needed.
verdict.correction: The claim is accurate as stated. The only clarification: `cluster-fluster.com` is not just for push notifications — it is the primary production API host for all CLI↔server communication (session sync, machine registration, RPC, auth). The webapp is at `app.happy.engineering`. Both are confirmed production endpoints.

verifications.claim: The dataKey AES-256-GCM variant is actually the default for new sessions (not just legacy accounts) — the encryption.md says 'Has dataKey?' but does not make clear when clients receive a dataKey from the server vs fall back to legacy secretbox.

verdict.claim: The dataKey AES-256-GCM variant is the default for new sessions (not just legacy accounts) — the encryption.md says 'Has dataKey?' but does not make clear when clients receive a dataKey from the server vs fall back to legacy secretbox.
verdict.evidence: The variant selection is entirely client-credential-driven, not server-driven, and the server does not push a dataKey to new clients. The decision branch is in `/packages/happy-cli/src/api/api.ts` lines 39-55: `if (this.credential.encryption.type === 'dataKey') { encryptionVariant = 'dataKey'; ... } else { encryptionVariant = 'legacy'; }`. Which credential type a client has is determined at auth time in `/packages/happy-cli/src/ui/auth.ts` lines 174-205: if the server response decrypts to exactly 32 bytes the client writes legacy credentials via `writeCredentialsLegacy()`; if it decrypts to a buffer whose first byte is `0` (version marker) the client writes dataKey credentials via `writeCredentialsDataKey()`. The server controls which format it returns in the auth response (the CLI sends `supportsV2: true` to signal capability). So: new accounts that authenticate against a server that has the V2 auth response implemented DO get `dataKey` credentials; accounts that authenticated before V2 or against an older server get `legacy`. The variant is therefore determined by what the server returned at auth-setup time (server-controlled, baked into the access.key file), not by a per-session flag from the server. The encryption.md diagram `Has dataKey?` refers to whether the locally stored Credentials have `encryption.type === 'dataKey'` — it is a client-local credential property, not a live server response. The claim that 'it is unclear when clients receive a dataKey' is refuted: clients receive the dataKey public key at initial authentication if and only if the server returns a V2 (`supportsV2: true`) response (first byte 0), and it is stored persistently in `access.key`. The claim that dataKey is not clearly the default for new sessions is also refuted: any account set up against a production server that supports V2 will use dataKey, making it the de-facto new-account default. Legacy secretbox applies only to accounts whose `access.key` was created before V2 or against a non-V2 server.
verdict.correction: The dataKey AES-256-GCM variant is the default for new accounts on a V2-capable server. It is not a per-session server push — it is determined once at initial CLI authentication. The server returns either a 32-byte legacy secret or a V2 bundle (first byte 0 + 32-byte public key) inside the encrypted auth response. The client inspects the decrypted length/prefix, writes the appropriate credential type to `~/.happy/access.key`, and all subsequent sessions for that credential use that variant permanently. The encryption.md diagram question 'Has dataKey?' means 'does the locally-stored access.key have `encryption.publicKey/machineKey`?' not 'did the server send a dataKey for this session?'. There is no server-to-client dataKey distribution at session creation time — the client generates a fresh 32-byte random session encryption key and wraps it with the pre-established public key before uploading to the server.

corpus.summary: happier-dev/happier is an independent rewrite/evolution that started as a fork of slopus/happy but diverged into a full-stack product. The host-side CLI (`happier` command, apps/cli) spawns the `claude` binary through TWO distinct codepaths: (a) "local mode" — direct PTY-passthrough spawn with stdin/stdout inherited so the interactive TUI stays live, plus a `--hook-plugin-dir` for a local HTTP hook server; (b) "remote/Agent-SDK mode" — spawns `claude --output-format stream-json --input-format stream-json` headlessly via `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` and relays structured JSON messages to clients over Socket.IO to a central relay server (`api.happier.dev` or self-hosted). The relay server is WebSocket-based (Socket.IO), self-hostable via Docker, and uses TweetNaCl (X25519+XSalsa20-Poly1305) for end-to-end encryption of session content. Auth is Happier's own account system (OAuth via GitHub/OIDC/mTLS) stored at `~/.happier`; Claude's own auth credentials (CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, etc.) are forwarded untouched into the spawned claude subprocess. The client app is React Native + Expo (iOS/Android/web) plus Tauri 2 for the desktop app.
corpus.hook_mechanism:
Two distinct hook mechanisms are used in parallel:

1. LOCAL MODE (PTY passthrough + Claude Code hooks):
`claudeLocal()` in `apps/cli/src/backends/claude/claudeLocal.ts:427` spawns the `claude` binary with `spawn(..., { stdio: ['inherit', 'inherit', 'inherit', 'pipe'] })` — stdin/stdout/stderr are all inherited so the full TUI is live in the terminal. A custom fd 3 is used as a side-channel for thinking-state tracking. Happier injects its config through three Claude CLI flags: `--plugin-dir <hookPluginDir>` (additive, does not collide with user wrappers like cmux), `--settings <hookSettingsPath>` (non-hook config, permissions.allow), and `--mcp-config <json>` (Happier's own MCP bridge). The `--plugin-dir` registers a `SessionStart` hook that POSTs to a local HTTP server (`startHookServer()` in `apps/cli/src/backends/claude/utils/startHookServer.ts`) on a random port, so Happier knows the active Claude session ID and transcript path without race conditions.

2. REMOTE/AGENT-SDK MODE (headless `--output-format stream-json`):
`claudeRemoteAgentSdk()` in `apps/cli/src/backends/claude/remote/claudeRemoteAgentSdk.ts:1` imports `query as agentSdkQuery` from `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` and spawns `claude --output-format stream-json --input-format stream-json`. Structured JSON messages stream over stdout/stdin. Happier wraps this in its own `Query` class (`apps/cli/src/backends/claude/sdk/query.ts:42`) that implements `AsyncIterableIterator<SDKMessage>`, handles `control_request` / `control_response` messages for permission callbacks, and fans out to the relay server. The dispatch layer (`claudeRemoteDispatch.ts`) tries Agent SDK first and falls back to a legacy `--print` codepath on auth or missing-executable errors.

The session scanner (`createSessionScanner`) watches the Claude JSONL transcript file on disk and streams parsed messages to clients in local mode, bridging local Claude I/O to the server without needing to intercept Claude's TUI output bytes.
corpus.architecture:
HOST SIDE (developer's machine):
- `happier` CLI daemon (`apps/cli`) — a Node.js/TypeScript process that acts as both the runner and the local gateway. It has two sub-personalities:
  - LOCAL mode: inherits Claude's stdin/stdout (full interactive TUI); simultaneously watches the JSONL transcript file and forwards parsed messages to the relay server.
  - REMOTE/AGENT-SDK mode: spawns Claude headlessly (`--output-format stream-json`), proxies structured JSON turns to relay server via Socket.IO.
- Local HTTP hook server (random port, localhost-only): receives Claude Code `SessionStart` and `PermissionRequest` hooks, relays to Happier's session tracking.
- Daemon process: background process (`apps/cli/src/daemon/`) that manages multiple concurrent sessions, machine-socket connection to relay, push-notification delivery, and tmux-backed persistent sessions.

RELAY SERVER:
- `apps/server` — TypeScript/Node.js server with Socket.IO transport, path `/v1/updates`. Self-hostable via Docker. Supports PostgreSQL, SQLite, MySQL. Default cloud: `api.happier.dev`. Multiple auth backends: GitHub OAuth, OIDC, mTLS.

CLIENT APPS:
- `apps/ui` — React Native + Expo (iOS App Store, Google Play, Android APK, web via react-native-web). Desktop via Tauri 2 (macOS, Windows, Linux). Both ship from the same codebase.

RELAY flow: CLI daemon ←→ Socket.IO ←→ relay server ←→ Socket.IO ←→ client app(s). Server stores E2E-encrypted session messages; keys never leave the devices.
corpus.transport_auth:
TRANSPORT: Socket.IO (WebSocket with polling fallback) between both the host CLI daemon and the relay server, authenticated with a machine-scoped JWT (`buildMachineScopedSocketAuth` from `@happier-dev/protocol`, sent as `auth` in the Socket.IO handshake). Path: `/v1/updates`.

ENCRYPTION: End-to-end using TweetNaCl (X25519 Diffie-Hellman + XSalsa20-Poly1305). Implemented in `packages/protocol/src/crypto/boxBundle.ts` (core primitive) and `packages/protocol/src/crypto/terminalProvisioningV2.ts` (terminal key provisioning). Key derivation uses SHA-512 (via `@noble/hashes`). The relay server stores only ciphertext; it cannot read session content. An account-scoped cipher (`accountScopedCipher.ts`) wraps message storage.

AUTH FOR HAPPIER ACCOUNT:
- Users authenticate to Happier's relay with their own account (OAuth via GitHub, OIDC providers, or mTLS). `happier auth login` opens a browser, generates PKCE, and stores a token in `~/.happier`.
- Self-hosted deployments also support GitHub-org/team gating, OIDC with per-provider email/group allowlists, and keyless external auth.
- Multiple server profiles supported simultaneously (`configuration.ts:1028` — `DEFAULT_SERVER_URL = 'https://api.happier.dev'`; overridable with `HAPPIER_SERVER_URL`).

AUTH FOR CLAUDE CODE:
Claude's own credentials are passed through verbatim into the spawned subprocess. `isolateClaudeRuntimeAuthEnv()` in `apps/cli/src/backends/claude/spawn/isolateClaudeRuntimeAuthEnv.ts` carefully filters which of `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `ANTHROPIC_OAUTH_TOKEN`, `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`, `CLAUDE_CODE_SETUP_TOKEN` etc. are forwarded, depending on which "connected service" profile is active. Happier does NOT touch or re-use the Claude OAuth session for its own relay auth — they are fully separate credential stores.
corpus.client_tech: React Native 0.81.5 + Expo 54 + Expo Router 6 for iOS, Android, and web (react-native-web 0.21). Tauri 2.8 for native desktop (macOS/Windows/Linux). Same codebase for all platforms. Notable native modules: `@more-tech/react-native-libsodium` for on-device crypto, `react-native-mmkv` for local storage, `@livekit/react-native` for WebRTC voice, Sentry for crash reporting, RevenueCat for subscriptions. Web terminal uses CodeMirror.

Over time, we realized that our own needs required faster iteration that we could not comfortably explore within the main project. So we started building them for ourselves.' The repository has its own complete monorepo with a fully separate server, protocol package, and client stack. No shared git history is evident. points.claim: Local mode is a genuine PTY passthrough — Claude's full TUI is rendered directly in the terminal, not captured and re-rendered.
points.detail: In claudeLocal.ts, the spawn call uses `stdio: ['inherit', 'inherit', 'inherit', 'pipe']`. stdin/stdout/stderr are all inherited (not piped), so Claude's Ink-based TUI renders directly in the host terminal. The 4th fd (pipe) is used only for the thinking-state side-channel (fetch-start/fetch-end JSON messages). Happier does NOT wrap Claude in a PTY multiplexer for local mode — it reads the transcript JSONL file separately for relay.

points.claim: Remote mode uses @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk with --output-format stream-json and --input-format stream-json.
points.detail: claudeRemoteAgentSdk.ts opens with `import { query as agentSdkQuery, ... } from '@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk'` and the query() function in sdk/query.ts builds args: `['--output-format', 'stream-json', '--verbose']` plus `['--input-format', 'stream-json']` when prompt is an AsyncIterable. points.claim: Claude Code's SessionStart hook is used (via --plugin-dir) to capture session IDs reliably, and PermissionRequest hook is used to gate tool approvals from the mobile UI.
points.detail: startHookServer.ts documents the full flow: happier launches a local HTTP server, generates a settings file with a SessionStart hook pointing to it, and passes --plugin-dir <path> to Claude. Claude fires the hook via a forwarder script (session_hook_forwarder.cjs), which POSTs session data to localhost:<port>/hook/session-start. PermissionRequest hooks follow the same pattern via /hook/permission-request, allowing the mobile UI to approve/deny tool calls.

points.claim: The relay server is fully self-hostable, documented, and supports multiple enterprise auth backends.
points.detail: README.md:211 describes GitHub OAuth with org/team gating, OIDC (Okta, any provider), mTLS, and keyless external auth. Docker Hub images at happierdev/relay-server. ProxmoxVE deployment docs included. Database backends: PostgreSQL, SQLite, MySQL. Default cloud relay is api.happier.dev.

points.claim: E2E encryption uses TweetNaCl X25519+XSalsa20-Poly1305 with ephemeral keys; server is architecturally zero-knowledge.
points.detail: boxBundle.ts:1 imports tweetnacl and uses tweetnacl.box() (X25519+XSalsa20-Poly1305) with ephemeral sender key pairs (a fresh random key is generated per sealed bundle). terminalProvisioningV2.ts seals the device's content private key to the recipient's public key for cross-device key provisioning. The relay stores only the ciphertext.

points.claim: Happier uses its own account/auth system entirely separate from Claude's OAuth. Claude's credentials pass through untouched into the subprocess env.
points.detail: authenticateClaudeSubscriptionOauth.ts shows Happier doing a full PKCE OAuth flow against claude.ai/oauth/authorize to obtain CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN — but this is for forwarding into the Claude subprocess, not for the relay. isolateClaudeRuntimeAuthEnv.ts then carefully injects only the allowed credential keys (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN depending on the 'connected service' profile) into the spawned claude child process env.

points.claim: Session resume uses Claude's own --resume flag, and tmux is used for persistent headless sessions.
points.detail: claudeLocal.ts intercepts --continue/-c and --resume/-r flags and translates them to Claude's native --resume <sessionId>. startHappyHeadlessInTmux.ts spawns the happier process inside a tmux window for persistence after SSH disconnect. The daemon tracks session IDs in its own state store so any device can resume.

points.claim: Push notifications for 'Claude is ready' are sent through the relay server to mobile clients.
points.detail: sendReadyWithPushNotification.ts is called from claudeRemoteLauncher when Claude finishes a turn and the queue is empty. It can include a preview of the assistant response text in the push body. The relay server dispatches to registered device push tokens. Configurable via AccountSettings.notificationsSettingsV1.

points.claim: The --settings flag conflict with PATH wrappers (e.g. cmux) is explicitly solved by using --plugin-dir for hooks instead.
points.detail: claudeLocal.ts:344 comment reads: '--settings is treated as a single overlay for the hooks field: when more than one --settings flag is passed, only the first wins... Any PATH-resident wrapper that prepends its own --settings... silently discards Happier's hook block. We carry non-hook config (permissions.allow) in --settings and move hooks to --plugin-dir, which is additive across multiple invocations so wrappers and Happier coexist.'

lessons: For PTY/TUI local mode: inherit stdio directly (as Happier does in claudeLocal.ts) rather than re-PTYing. This preserves Claude's full TUI. Use a separate fd (pipe) or a JSONL file watcher for the relay side-channel, not stdout interception.

Use Claude Code's --plugin-dir for hook injection rather than --settings. The --settings flag is last-write-wins and PATH wrappers (cmux, etc.) will silently drop it. --plugin-dir is additive. This is the production-hardened lesson Happier documented explicitly.

Use the Claude Code SessionStart hook (via --plugin-dir/hooks.json) to learn the session ID and transcript path from Claude itself. File-watching has race conditions when multiple processes run concurrently. The hook gives a 1:1 process-to-session guarantee.

The Agent SDK / --output-format stream-json path is the right choice for 'remote mode' (where the UI lives on another device). It provides structured JSON turns without the noise of ANSI escape sequences from the TUI, and allows interrupt/resume via stdin control_request messages.

E2E encrypt session messages at the application layer (TweetNaCl / libsodium) before they touch the relay. The relay becomes zero-knowledge. This is critical for developer trust — source code and auth tokens transit the relay.

For our PTY-over-NetBird approach: we can bypass the relay server entirely (P2P advantage). But Happier's model of keeping Claude auth entirely separate from transport auth is worth copying — pass Claude's CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN into the subprocess env, maintain our own connection auth layer.

tmux as the persistence layer for headless daemon sessions is proven. Happier uses it for sessions started outside the app. We can do the same: store the PTY in a tmux pane, and attach/detach libghostty clients independently.

Push notifications on 'Claude ready' with a preview of the last assistant message are a high-value UX feature. The relay server dispatches these on turn completion. In our P2P model, we could deliver this via APNs/FCM triggered by the host process directly.

The local/remote mode switch (user presses double-space to take over in terminal) is a valuable UX pattern. In our architecture the equivalent is attaching the libghostty client directly to the host PTY — we should support both headless (mobile sends prompts) and attached (desktop takes over the PTY) modes.

Happier's approach of a 'connected services' layer that materializes ANTHROPIC_API_KEY vs. CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN selectively into the child env is worth adapting. It handles the case where the user may have multiple accounts or auth modes.

FULL ARCHITECTURE FINDINGS (included here for the parent agent since the schema only captures the adversarial verdict):

## slopus/happy

**Architecture:** Three-tier — CLI wrapper on host machine, relay server (Fastify + Socket.IO + Postgres/Redis/S3), Expo mobile/web app.

**How it hooks Claude Code — two modes:**

*Local/interactive mode* (`claudeLocal.ts`): Spawns the `claude` binary via `crossSpawn` with `stdio: ['inherit', 'inherit', 'inherit', 'pipe']`. This means Claude Code runs with the full TUI visible in the terminal — stdin/stdout are inherited (passed through to the user's TTY), not intercepted. Happy attaches to fd 3 (a custom extra pipe) which the `claude_local_launcher.cjs` wrapper uses to emit JSON `fetch-start`/`fetch-end` events for "thinking" state tracking. There is NO byte-stream interception of the TUI — Claude Code runs normally and the user sees the terminal UI.

*Remote/headless mode* (`claudeRemote.ts` + `sdk/query.ts`): Spawns Claude Code with `--output-format stream-json --verbose --input-format stream-json`. This is the Claude Agent SDK headless mode. Messages flow as newline-delimited JSON over stdout/stdin pipes. The `Query` class reads stdout line-by-line and parses SDK message types (`user`, `assistant`, `tool_use`, `tool_result`, `system`, `control_request`). This is mechanism (b) — headless `claude -p --output-format stream-json`.

*Session tracking via Claude Code Hooks:* In both modes, happy injects a `--settings` file (written to `~/.happy/tmp/hooks/session-hook-<pid>.json`) that registers a `SessionStart` hook command pointing to a local HTTP server (`startHookServer.ts`). When Claude fires the hook, a forwarder script POSTs the session data to `http://127.0.0.1:<random-port>/hook/session-start`, and happy uses the `session_id` to correlate the Claude Code session with its own server-side session record.

*MCP:* An in-process `happy` MCP server (`startHappyServer`) is injected via `--mcp-config` with type `http`. This exposes RPC tools (bash execution, file read/write, ripgrep, difftastic) to Claude Code as MCP tools.

**Transport:** Socket.IO over WebSocket to the relay server (`/v1/updates`). ## happier-dev/happier

**Architecture:** Same three-tier pattern but significantly more elaborate. Relay server (Fastify + Socket.IO + Postgres or SQLite + Redis optional + S3 or local files), Machine Daemon (long-running background process per machine), CLI wrapper, Expo + Tauri client (iOS/Android/web/desktop).

**How it hooks Claude Code:**

*For Claude Code specifically — two modes, nearly identical to slopus/happy since happier forked from it:*

*Local/interactive mode* (`claudeLocal.ts`): Spawns `claude` via Node's `spawn` with `stdio: ['inherit', 'inherit', 'inherit', 'pipe']`. Same TUI pass-through as happy. fd 3 monitored for thinking-state JSON events from `claude_local_launcher.cjs`.

*Remote/headless mode* (`claudeRemote.ts` + `sdk/query.ts`): Spawns Claude with `--output-format stream-json --verbose --input-format stream-json`. The `Query` class reads stdout line by line. ## Adversarial Verdict: SessionStart hook schema stability

The claim is CONFIRMED. The `startHookServer.ts` in happier-dev/happier defensively parses both `session_id` (snake_case) and `sessionId` (camelCase), plus `transcript_path` AND `transcriptPath`, while the slopus/happy version only handles `session_id`/`sessionId` and `transcript_path` (no `transcriptPath`). This camelCase-vs-snake_case dual handling, combined with the fact that happier added `transcriptPath` as an additional alias that slopus/happy does not have, is direct evidence that the schema has varied across Claude Code versions. The `generateHookSettings.ts` comment also documents that the DELIVERY CHANNEL for hooks (not just the payload) changed — from `--settings` to `--plugin-dir` — because the `--settings` mechanism silently dropped hooks when a PATH-resident wrapper claimed the slot first. Both the payload schema and the registration mechanism have evolved.

## Lessons for PTY-over-NetBird + libghostty approach

**Borrow from both projects:**

1. SessionStart hook for session ID correlation: Both projects register a Claude Code `SessionStart` hook to learn the canonical Claude session UUID. Use `--plugin-dir` (not `--settings`) to avoid silent hook drops from PATH wrappers. Parse both snake_case and camelCase defensively.

2. Dual-mode architecture: Interactive (PTY passthrough) + headless (`--output-format stream-json`) for different use cases. Happy/happier switch between them at runtime. The PTY mode is exactly your approach; the SDK mode enables non-interactive/autonomous operation.

3. E2E encryption before the relay: Both projects encrypt client-side and store opaque blobs. Even if NetBird provides transport security, encrypting session content at the application layer means the relay (or NetBird peer) cannot read transcripts.

4. Offline/reconnection resilience: Both maintain a local session scanner that watches the Claude JSONL transcript file on disk. If the relay connection drops, the local process keeps running and syncs when reconnected.

5. Auth flow: Reuse Claude Code's own OAuth subscription — do not try to replace or duplicate it. Both projects call `claude auth login` as a prerequisite and store the resulting credential; they do not issue their own Anthropic API keys to the CLI.

**Your approach is different/better in:**

1. P2P transport (NetBird/WireGuard): Neither project is P2P. Both require a relay server as a broker. NetBird eliminates the relay server for the byte-stream path, reducing latency and removing a trust boundary for the TUI byte stream. However, you will still need some coordination service for device discovery, push notifications, and session metadata persistence (unless you embed that in the host daemon).

2. Full TUI fidelity via libghostty: Both happy/happier in local mode pass stdin/stdout through to the host terminal (PTY inherit). They do NOT stream the TUI to the mobile client — the mobile client gets a structured JSON message stream (SDK mode) or RPC-driven session data, not the actual terminal bytes. Your approach streams the raw PTY bytes over NetBird and renders them natively in libghostty, giving exact TUI fidelity including colors, cursor, selection, etc.

**Pitfalls to watch for (from their code):**

1. `--settings` is non-composable. If you inject Claude Code configuration via `--settings`, PATH-resident wrappers silently win. Use `--plugin-dir` for hooks.

2. SessionStart hook schema instability: Parse both `session_id` and `sessionId`, both `transcript_path` and `transcriptPath`. Claude Code has shipped both forms.

3. stdin blocking mode: `claudeLocal.ts` in slopus/happy explicitly calls `stdinHandle.setBlocking(true)` before spawning Claude to clear `O_NONBLOCK` left by Node's libuv. On macOS/Linux this causes garbled echo and duplicated cursors (issue slopus/happy#301). You will hit this with PTY inheritance.

4. `CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT` env var: The Claude Code `--resume` picker filters out sessions whose entrypoint is in the SDK set (`sdk-cli`, `sdk-ts`, `sdk-py`). Set `CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT=remote_mobile` (or another non-SDK value) when spawning in SDK/headless mode so the user can still `claude --resume` those sessions from their terminal.

5. Duplicate prompt forwarding: When the app sends a message AND the user types in the terminal, both end up in the Claude JSONL. Both projects maintain a dedup ring buffer keyed on message text + timestamp to avoid double-forwarding user prompts. Plan for this if you expose both a compose box and a PTY simultaneously.

The only refinement: the schema instability covers not just `session_id` vs `sessionId` but also `transcript_path` vs `transcriptPath` and the entire hook delivery channel (from `--settings` to `--plugin-dir`), making the instability broader than stated in the original claim.
